title: Definitions
content: This section provides essential definitions used throughout the Act. Key terms include: 'invention' means a new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application; 'inventive step' means a feature that involves technical advance or has economic significance or both, making the invention not obvious to a person skilled in the art; 'patent' means a patent for any invention granted under this Act; 'patentee' means the person entered on the register as the grantee or proprietor of the patent; 'patent agent' means a person registered as a patent agent under this Act; 'new invention' means any invention or technology which has not been anticipated by publication in any document or used in the country or elsewhere in the world before the date of filing; 'patent office' means the patent office referred to in Section 74; 'pharmaceutical substance' means any new entity involving one or more inventive steps.

title: What Are Not Inventions
content: This section enumerates categories of subject matter that are not considered inventions within the meaning of the Act and therefore cannot be patented. These include: an invention which is frivolous or contrary to well-established natural laws; an invention the primary or intended use of which would be contrary to public order or morality or which causes serious prejudice to human, animal or plant life or health or to the environment; the mere discovery of a scientific principle or formulation of an abstract theory or discovery of any living thing or non-living substance occurring in nature; the mere discovery of a new form of a known substance which does not result in enhancement of the known efficacy of that substance (Section 3(d)); a method of agriculture or horticulture; any process for the medicinal, surgical, curative, prophylactic, diagnostic, therapeutic or other treatment of human beings or animals; plants and animals in whole or any part thereof other than micro-organisms; a mathematical or business method or computer programme per se or algorithms; a literary, dramatic, musical or artistic work; a mere scheme or rule or method of performing mental act or method of playing game; a presentation of information; topography of integrated circuits; an invention which is traditional knowledge or an aggregation or duplication of known properties.

title: Inventions Relating to Atomic Energy Not Patentable
content: No patent shall be granted in respect of an invention relating to atomic energy falling within sub-section (1) of Section 20 of the Atomic Energy Act, 1962. This provision ensures that inventions concerning atomic energy remain under the exclusive domain of the government in the interest of national security and public safety. Any application for a patent that relates to the production, control, use or disposal of atomic energy or the prospecting, mining, extraction, production, physical and chemical treatment, fabrication, enrichment, canning or use of any prescribed substance is excluded from patent protection under this Act.

title: Inventions Where Only Methods or Processes of Manufacture Patentable (Repealed)
content: This section was originally enacted to restrict patents in the food, chemical, and pharmaceutical sectors to only methods or processes of manufacture, and not to the substances themselves. This was a key provision that distinguished Indian patent law from many Western countries by denying product patents for food, medicines, and chemicals. The section was repealed by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005, which introduced product patents in all fields of technology in compliance with the TRIPS Agreement obligations under the WTO. The repeal marked a significant shift in Indian patent law towards harmonization with international standards.

title: Persons Entitled to Apply for Patents
content: An application for a patent for an invention may be made by any of the following persons, that is to say: (a) by any person claiming to be the true and first inventor of the invention; (b) by any person being the assignee of the person claiming to be the true and first inventor in respect of the right to make such an application; (c) by the legal representative of any deceased person who immediately before his death was entitled to make such an application. An application may be made by any such person either alone or jointly with any other person. The expression 'true and first inventor' does not include either the first importer of an invention into India or a person to whom an invention is first communicated from outside India.

title: Form of Application
content: Every application for a patent shall be made in the prescribed form and filed in the patent office. The application must contain a specification, an abstract of the invention, drawings (if necessary), and such other particulars as may be prescribed. The application shall state that the applicant is in possession of the invention and shall name the person claiming to be the true and first inventor. Where the applicant is not the true and first inventor, the application shall contain a declaration naming the inventor and stating how the applicant derived the right to make the application. Every international application under the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) designating India shall be deemed to be an application under this Act.

title: Information and Undertaking Regarding Foreign Applications
content: Where an applicant for a patent under this Act is prosecuting either alone or jointly with any other person an application for a patent in any country outside India in respect of the same or substantially the same invention, the applicant shall file along with his application a statement setting out the particulars of such application. The applicant must also provide an undertaking that, up to the date of grant of patent in India, he shall keep the Controller informed in writing of the detailed particulars of the nature and outcome of all applications filed outside India relating to the same or substantially the same invention. Non-compliance with this provision can be a ground for opposition or revocation of the patent.

title: Provisional and Complete Specification
content: Where an application for a patent is accompanied by a provisional specification, a complete specification shall be filed within twelve months from the date of filing of the application. If the complete specification is not filed within the prescribed time, the application shall be deemed to be abandoned. A provisional specification describes the nature of the invention and need not contain claims, whereas the complete specification must fully and particularly describe the invention, its operation and the manner in which it is to be performed, and must end with a claim or claims defining the scope of the invention. The applicant may also file a complete specification along with the application itself instead of a provisional specification.

title: Contents of Specification
content: Every specification, whether provisional or complete, shall describe the invention and shall begin with a title sufficiently indicating the subject matter to which the invention relates. The complete specification shall fully and particularly describe the invention and its operation or use and the method by which it is to be performed. It shall disclose the best method of performing the invention known to the applicant at the time of filing. The complete specification shall end with a claim or claims defining the scope of the invention for which protection is claimed. The claims shall be clear, succinct, and fairly based on the matter disclosed in the specification. If the specification relates to a biological material which may not be described in a manner to satisfy the disclosure requirements, the applicant shall deposit the material at an international depository authority.

title: Publication of Applications
content: No application for patent shall ordinarily be open to the public for a period of eighteen months from the date of filing or the date of priority of the application, whichever is earlier. After the expiry of eighteen months, the Controller shall publish the application in the prescribed manner. Upon publication, the applicant shall have the like privileges and rights as if a patent had been granted on the date of publication. However, the applicant shall not be entitled to institute any proceedings for infringement until the patent has been granted. The applicant may request early publication before the expiry of the eighteen-month period by making a request in writing.

title: Examination of Application
content: No application for a patent shall be examined unless the applicant or any other interested person makes a request for examination within the prescribed period of forty-eight months from the date of priority or the date of filing, whichever is earlier. Upon receipt of such request, the Controller shall refer the application and specification along with related documents to an examiner. The examiner shall make a report to the Controller ordinarily within a period of one to three months. If no request for examination is made within the prescribed period, the application shall be treated as withdrawn.

title: Opposition to the Patent
content: Section 25(1) provides for pre-grant opposition where any person may represent by way of opposition to the Controller against the grant of a patent on various grounds including prior publication, prior claim, prior public knowledge or use, obviousness, non-patentable subject matter under Sections 3 and 4, insufficiency of description, non-disclosure of information under Section 8, or that the invention was convention-barred. Section 25(2) provides for post-grant opposition where any interested person may give notice of opposition within one year from the date of publication of the grant on similar grounds. Pre-grant opposition is decided by the Controller, while post-grant opposition is decided by the Opposition Board constituted for the purpose.

title: Residents Not to Apply for Patents Outside India Without Prior Permission
content: No person resident in India shall, except under the authority of a written permit granted by the Controller, make or cause to be made any application outside India for the grant of a patent for an invention unless an application for patent for the same invention has been made in India not less than six weeks before the application outside India and either no direction has been given under Section 35 relating to secrecy or all such directions have been revoked. Contravention of this provision is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both, and any patent granted on such application shall be void.

title: Rights of Patentees
content: Subject to other provisions of this Act, a patent granted under this Act shall confer upon the patentee the exclusive right to prevent third parties who do not have his consent from the act of making, using, offering for sale, selling or importing for those purposes the patented product in India, where the subject matter of the patent is a product. Where the subject matter of the patent is a process, the patentee has the exclusive right to prevent third parties from using that process, and from using, offering for sale, selling or importing for those purposes the product obtained directly by that process in India.

title: Term of Patent
content: Subject to the provisions of this Act, every patent granted under this Act shall be for a term of twenty years from the date of filing of the application for the patent. The patent shall cease to have effect on the expiry of the period of twenty years from the date of filing. For patents granted on applications made under the Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT), the term of twenty years begins from the international filing date. The patent term cannot be extended beyond twenty years. If the renewal fees are not paid within the prescribed period, the patent shall cease to have effect, though restoration is possible within eighteen months.

title: Patents of Addition
content: Where an application is made for a patent in respect of any improvement in or modification of an invention described or disclosed in the specification of a patent (main invention) already applied for or granted, the Controller may, if the applicant so requests, grant the patent for the improvement or modification as a patent of addition. A patent of addition shall not be granted before the grant of the patent for the main invention. A patent of addition shall remain in force so long as the patent for the main invention remains in force, and no separate renewal fee is payable for the patent of addition.

title: Revocation of Patents
content: A patent may be revoked on a petition by any person interested or by the Central Government or on a counter-claim in a suit for infringement, on various grounds including that the invention was not new, that the patent was obtained wrongfully, that the patentee failed to disclose required information, that the invention was obvious, that the subject matter is not patentable under Sections 3 or 4, that the specification does not sufficiently describe the invention, or that the patent was obtained by fraud or misrepresentation. The Appellate Board (now the High Court, post-2021 tribunal reform) may revoke the patent on being satisfied of the existence of any of the grounds.

title: Compulsory Licences
content: At any time after the expiration of three years from the date of the grant of a patent, any person interested may make an application to the Controller for grant of a compulsory licence on the grounds that the reasonable requirements of the public with respect to the patented invention have not been satisfied, or that the patented invention is not available to the public at a reasonably affordable price, or that the patented invention is not worked in the territory of India. The Controller shall consider the nature of the invention, the time elapsed since the grant, measures taken by the patentee to make full use of the invention, and the ability of the applicant to work the invention to the public advantage.

title: Special Provision for Compulsory Licences on Notifications by Central Government
content: If the Central Government is satisfied in respect of any patent in force that it is necessary in circumstances of national emergency or in circumstances of extreme urgency or in case of public non-commercial use, it may issue a notification in the Official Gazette directing the Controller to grant compulsory licences on such terms and conditions as it may deem fit. The Controller shall, upon receipt of such notification, grant to every person making an application a licence under the patent on such terms and conditions as the Controller deems appropriate. This provision was notably invoked in the context of the Doha Declaration on TRIPS and public health.

title: Jurisdiction
content: No suit for a declaration under Section 105 or for any relief under Section 106 or for infringement of a patent shall be instituted in any court inferior to a District Court having jurisdiction to try the suit. For the purposes of clauses (a) and (b) of Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, a District Court within whose jurisdiction the person who is sued or any of the persons who are sued actually and voluntarily resides or carries on business or personally works for gain shall have jurisdiction. The suit for infringement of a patent can be filed in the District Court or the High Court having jurisdiction.

title: Certain Acts Not to Be Considered as Infringement
content: The following acts shall not be considered as infringement of patent rights: (a) any act of making, constructing, using, selling or importing a patented invention solely for uses reasonably related to the development and submission of information required under any law for the time being in force, in India, or in a country other than India, that regulates the manufacture, construction, use, sale or import of any product (known as the Bolar provision); (b) importation of patented products by any person from a person who is duly authorised under the law to produce and sell or distribute the product (parallel importation).
